title: Projects
text:
Work
  Projects
  
    Applied research, teaching tools, and data storytelling experiments.
    Each project is built around a real methodological question.
  




Data Storytelling · Teaching Module

Hip-Hop Database


A fully documented dataset of 138 hip-hop artists scored across five lyricism dimensions, enriched with Billboard chart data, award counts, career metrics, and a custom atomic weight formula. Built to teach measurement validity, sampling bias, composite scoring, and uncertainty communication — using a subject matter executives actually argue about. Includes an interactive periodic table and a multi-tab analytical dashboard.


R / Quarto HTML / JS Excel / readxl Interactive

  View project → 




More projects coming soon.

title: The Hip-HopDatabase
The Hip-HopDatabase
text:
What’s in this project


⬡

The Periodic Table


138 artists arranged as chemical elements — grouped by lyrical style,
ordered by era, assigned a unique atomic weight. Click any element
for the full artist profile. Filter by region, era, or confidence tier.

Open →


📊

The Analytical Dashboard


Six interactive tabs walking through construct validity, sampling bias,
the aggregation problem, uncertainty communication, and visualization
design — all using the same underlying dataset.

Open →


📁

The Excel Database


The living source file: 40 columns including five lyricism dimension scores,
Billboard chart data, Grammy and award counts, career span, platinum albums,
atomic weight formula, deceased status, and confidence flags.

Download .xlsx →





Methodology

title: The Hip-HopDatabase
The Hip-HopDatabase
text:
The atomic weight
Every artist has a unique atomic weight — a continuous value that works like an element’s atomic number, but is calculated rather than assigned. It can accommodate new artists without renumbering everyone else.

Atomic Weight = (Career Span × Composite Score) + Total Awards                   + (Birth Year ÷ 100,000) + (Hot 100 Entries ÷ 10,000,000)  — The last two terms are tiebreakers only, invisible at 4 decimal places. — Career Span = Last album year − Debut year (minimum 1). — Total Awards = Grammy wins + BET Hip-Hop wins + MTV VMA wins + AMA wins.

The formula rewards sustained artistic output over raw peak performance. Rakim (#1 at 281.62) has no Grammys and modest chart presence — he ranks first because a 32-year career multiplied by an 8.8 composite accumulates more weight than a short brilliant run. Biggie, with a 9.0 composite and only a 4-year career, sits at 38.02. That tension is the point.



Transparency

title: The Hip-HopDatabase
text:
Documented uncertainty
Every artist carries a confidence flag — High, Medium, or Low — reflecting the quality of critical documentation available when the lyricism scores were assigned.




Flag


Meaning


Example artists






H


Substantial academic and critical documentation. Scores are well-grounded.


Kendrick Lamar, Rakim, Nas, Eminem, OutKast




M


Moderate documentation. Scores are reasonable but provisional.


Cam’ron, J.I.D, Doechii, Smino, Maxo Kream




L


Thin critical record. Scores are estimates only — treat with caution.


GloRilla, Mavi, Zach Fox, Yaya Bey, Dana Dane






Teaching concept — Communicating Uncertainty


Most dashboards present numbers without confidence levels. This one doesn't.
Where in your organization's reporting does uncertainty get hidden?
What decisions are being made on L-confidence data presented as H-confidence?




Built with R · Quarto · readxl · ggplot2 · plotly · reactable

title: Data with a Plot
text:
About


LEW


I work at the intersection of data, law, and organizational decision-making. My non-work work focuses on making rigorous analytical thinking accessible — through unexpected datasets, honest methodology, and visualizations that show their own uncertainty.

PhD — [Social Psychology, certificate in Research Methods and Data Analysis] JD — [specialized in Health Law] [Senior Research Associate / National Center for State Courts]

I’m particularly interested in the gap between how organizations say they make decisions and how they actually make decisions — and in the role that poorly designed measurement plays in that gap.
When I’m not doing that, I’m building models of hip-hop artists.

title: Data with a Plot
text:
What I do


Teaching
Executive and graduate education in data storytelling, measurement design, and evidence-based decision-making. I use real datasets with documented flaws — because clean pedagogical data teaches the wrong lessons.

Research
Applied work at the boundary of law, psychology, and data storytelling. Particular interest in construct validity, uncertainty quantification, and the politics of how metrics get built and used.

Projects
Independent projects that translate methodological ideas into interactive, shareable artifacts. The hip-hop table is one. More in the projects gallery.




Built with Quarto · Hosted on GitHub Pages
